title: Fiscal, monetary & supply-side policy
summary:
## The big idea Diagnosis done (Unit 4), objectives set (last chapter) — now the treatment. Governments own three toolkits. **Fiscal policy** (the budget) and **monetary policy** (the central bank) are **demand-side**: they shift **AD**. **Supply-side policy** shifts **LRAS** — the economy's capacity itself. The exam game is always the same: pick the tool that matches the cause, and evaluate it honestly. ## 1. Fiscal policy — the government's budget **Fiscal policy** uses **government spending (G)** and **taxation (T)** to steer AD. - **Expansionary**: raise G and/or cut T → households and firms have more to spend → **AD shifts right**. Used against recession and cyclical unemployment. Usually widens the **budget deficit** (G > T), which accumulates into **national debt**. - **Contractionary**: cut G and/or raise T → **AD shifts left**. Used to cool demand-pull inflation (and repair the public finances). A tax cut works partly through the **multiplier**: the first round of extra spending becomes someone's income, which is partly spent again, and so on — the final rise in AD exceeds the initial injection. **Strengths:** can be targeted (at regions, the poor, infrastructure); G works even when confidence is so low that cheap credit is ignored; capital spending doubles as supply-side policy. **Weaknesses:** **time lags** (budgets are annual; roads take years); rising **debt** and interest costs; **crowding out** — government borrowing may push up interest rates and displace private investment; political temptations (giveaways before elections). ## 2. Monetary policy — the central bank **Monetary policy** uses **interest rates** (and the money supply / **quantitative easing**) to steer AD, usually to hit an **inflation target** (~2%). Follow the transmission chain for a **rate cut**: borrowing is cheaper and saving pays less → **C rises** (cheaper mortgages and credit) and **I rises** (more projects profitable) → also, lower returns make the currency less attractive → **depreciation** → exports cheaper → **(X−M) rises**. All three push **AD right**. A rate **rise** runs the whole chain in reverse to cool inflation. **Strengths:** flexible (rates can change monthly), run by an **independent central bank** — credibility keeps inflation expectations anchored; no direct budget cost. **Weaknesses:** long, uncertain **lags** (up to ~18 months); blunt — the same rate hits every region and sector, punishing borrowers and exporters; weak at very low rates (the **liquidity trap**: after 2008, rates near zero still couldn't spark spending, forcing QE); depends on banks passing rates on and on confidence. ## 3. Supply-side policy — growing the machine **Supply-side policies** raise **productive capacity and efficiency**, shifting **LRAS right**. This is potential growth — the only route to higher output *without* inflation in the long run. Two flavours: - **Market-based** (free the market): cut income and corporation taxes to sharpen incentives, **deregulation**, **privatisation**, loosening restrictive labour laws, trimming benefits to encourage work. - **Interventionist** (state builds capacity): spending on **education and training**, **infrastructure**, healthcare, and **R&D** to raise human capital and productivity.
**What each part means:** capacity grows — better skills, more infrastructure, new technology — so the vertical long-run supply curve shifts from **LRAS1** to the dashed **LRAS2**. With demand (**AD**) unchanged, equilibrium moves from the grey dot to the orange one: real GDP **rises from Y1 to Y2** while the price level **falls from P1 to P2**. Compare that with demand-side stimulus, which raises output *and* prices — this is why supply-side success is the policymaker's dream outcome.
**Strengths:** uniquely, can improve **all four objectives at once** — more growth, more jobs, downward pressure on prices, better competitiveness (trade balance). **Weaknesses:** very **long time lags** (educating a workforce takes a generation); expensive (interventionist) or painful for equity (market-based cuts to taxes and benefits tend to widen inequality); success is uncertain — deregulation can end in crisis (finance, 2008). ## Matching tool to problem - **Cyclical unemployment / recession** → expansionary fiscal and/or monetary policy. - **Demand-pull inflation** → contractionary demand-side policy. - **Cost-push inflation** → demand squeezes just deepen the slump; supply-side (and exchange-rate) remedies fit better. - **Structural unemployment / weak long-run growth** → supply-side. Real economies mix them: after 2008 and again in 2020, most governments combined near-zero interest rates, QE *and* fiscal stimulus — then spent the 2020s arguing about the resulting debt and inflation.
## ⚡ Quick revision - **Fiscal** = G & T (demand-side). Expansionary → AD right, deficit widens; watch lags, debt, **crowding out**. - **Monetary** = interest rates/QE (demand-side). Rate cut → C↑, I↑, currency depreciates → AD right; lags ~18 months, **liquidity trap** near zero. - **Supply-side** = LRAS right: **market-based** (tax cuts, deregulation, privatisation) vs **interventionist** (education, infrastructure, R&D). - Only LRAS growth raises output **without** inflation — but slowly and expensively. - Match the tool to the **cause**: demand shock → demand-side; structural weakness → supply-side. - Top-band answers weigh strengths *and* weaknesses and name the trade-offs.

keyTerms.definition: Using government spending and taxation to influence aggregate demand.
When government spending exceeds tax revenue in a period; adds to national debt.
A central bank's use of interest rates and the money supply to influence AD.
The price/reward of borrowing and saving money; the main monetary-policy tool.
Policies to raise productive capacity and efficiency, shifting LRAS right.
When higher government borrowing/spending reduces private-sector investment.
Demand-side policy shifts AD; supply-side policy shifts LRAS.

diagrams: **Expansionary demand-side policy:** AD shifts right on AD/AS → higher output (and possibly higher price level near capacity).
**Contractionary policy:** AD shifts left → lower price level and output.
**Supply-side policy:** LRAS (and SRAS) shift right → higher output with a *lower* price level — the ideal outcome.
tips: Classify tools correctly: **fiscal & monetary = demand-side (shift AD)**; **supply-side = shift LRAS**.
For every policy, give **strengths AND weaknesses** (time lags, debt, crowding out, uncertainty) to reach the top bands.
Match the **policy to the cause**: demand-side for cyclical problems, supply-side for structural/long-run growth.
Stress that only **supply-side/LRAS growth** raises output *without* inflation in the long run — a key evaluation line.
Note the **conflicts/side-effects** (e.g. supply-side reforms raising inequality; higher rates hurting exporters).

channels.name: tutor2u Economics
channels.why: Comprehensive fiscal, monetary and supply-side coverage.

revisionNotes:
# Fiscal, monetary & supply-side policy — quick revision

**Fiscal (budget: G and T) — demand-side**
- Expansionary: ↑G / ↓T → AD right (fight recession); widens **budget deficit** → national debt.
- Contractionary: reverse (fight demand-pull inflation).
- + Targeted; works when confidence is dead; capital G doubles as supply-side.
- − Time lags; debt & interest costs; **crowding out**; politics.

**Monetary (central bank: interest rates, QE) — demand-side**
- Rate cut chain: cheaper borrowing → **C↑, I↑**; currency **depreciates** → **(X−M)↑** → AD right. Rate rise = reverse.
- + Flexible; independent central bank credibility; no budget cost.
- − ~18-month lags; blunt; **liquidity trap** at near-zero rates (→ QE); needs confidence.

**Supply-side — shifts LRAS right (potential growth)**
- **Market-based**: income/corporation tax cuts, deregulation, privatisation, labour-market reform.
- **Interventionist**: education & training, infrastructure, health, R&D.
- + Can improve growth, jobs, inflation *and* trade at once; no inflation in the long run.
- − Long lags, costly, uncertain; market-based variants raise inequality.

**Matching**
- Recession/cyclical U → expansionary demand-side. Demand-pull inflation → contractionary. Cost-push → supply-side. Structural problems → supply-side.

**Exam habit** — for each policy: mechanism (with AD/AS diagram) → strengths → weaknesses → judgement matched to the cause.
questions:
# Practice questions — Fiscal, monetary & supply-side policy **Original practice questions in the Cambridge 9708 style** (not copied from real papers). Attempt each, reveal the answer, then self-mark. Links to real past papers are in the Past-paper questions panel. ## Paper 1 style — multiple choice **1.** Which of the following is an example of expansionary fiscal policy? - A — the central bank lowering interest rates - B — the government increasing spending on road building - C — a fall in the exchange rate - D — banks increasing their lending
▸ Show answer

B. Fiscal policy = government spending and taxation. A is monetary policy; C and D are not policy decisions by the government at all.

**2.** A central bank raises interest rates. The most likely effects are: - A — consumption rises and the currency depreciates - B — investment rises and exports increase - C — consumption and investment fall and the currency appreciates - D — aggregate supply shifts left
▸ Show answer

C. Dearer borrowing cuts C and I, while higher returns attract foreign funds → appreciation (further reducing AD via net exports). The whole chain works to cool demand.

**3.** Supply-side policies differ from demand-side policies because they aim primarily to: - A — shift the AD curve to the right - B — reduce the budget deficit - C — increase the economy's productive capacity - D — reduce the exchange rate
▸ Show answer

C. Supply-side policy shifts LRAS right — raising capacity and potential output — rather than manipulating spending.

**4.** 'Crowding out' occurs when: - A — higher government borrowing raises interest rates and displaces private investment - B — inflation reduces the real value of savings - C — imports displace domestically produced goods - D — an independent central bank ignores government wishes
▸ Show answer

A. The government competes with firms for loanable funds; the resulting higher interest rates squeeze out some private-sector investment, offsetting part of the fiscal stimulus.

## Paper 2 style — data response > An economy is in recession: unemployment has risen to 8%, inflation is 1%, and factories report weak order books. The policy interest rate is already at 0.5%. The budget deficit stands at 6% of GDP and the finance minister worries that 'markets will punish more borrowing'. An adviser proposes a five-year programme of school-building and vocational training instead. **(a)** Define 'monetary policy'. **[2]** **(b)** Explain **two** reasons why cutting interest rates further may fail to lift aggregate demand in this economy. **[4]** **(c)** Analyse, with the aid of an AD/AS diagram, how the school-building and training programme could affect the economy in both the short run and the long run. **[6]** **(d)** Assess which policy approach this government should choose. **[8]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) The central bank's use of interest rates (and money supply/QE) to influence AD, typically to hit an inflation target. [1+1]

(b) (i) Liquidity trap: at 0.5% there is little room to cut, and savers/banks may hoard anyway; (ii) confidence: firms won't borrow to invest when order books are weak whatever the rate (also acceptable: banks not passing cuts on). [2+2]

(c) Short run: construction spending = expansionary fiscal policy → AD right → output and jobs (strong effect given slack). Long run: better-skilled workforce and infrastructure → LRAS right → higher potential output with downward price pressure. One diagram showing both shifts scores well. [6]

(d) Monetary: nearly exhausted. Fiscal worry: 6% deficit — but borrowing costs are low at 0.5%, and capital spending raises future capacity (and tax revenue) which reassures markets more than consumption giveaways. Judgement: the investment programme (fiscal + supply-side hybrid) fits the diagnosis; note the debt risk honestly. [8]

## Paper 2 / 4 style — essays **Essay 1 (a)** Explain how expansionary fiscal policy and expansionary monetary policy each increase aggregate demand. **[8]** **(b)** Assess whether monetary policy is the best way to control inflation. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Fiscal: ↑G directly, ↓T → disposable income → C (multiplier). Monetary: rate cut → C, I, depreciation → X−M. Diagrams welcome.

(b) For: independent, credible, flexible, proven against demand-pull inflation. Against: lags, blunt, weak vs cost-push inflation (raising rates against an oil shock deepens recession), hurts borrowers/exporters. Judgement: best for demand-pull with anchored expectations; needs fiscal/supply-side partners otherwise.

**Essay 2 (a)** Explain the difference between market-based and interventionist supply-side policies, with examples. **[8]** **(b)** Discuss whether supply-side policies are the most effective way for a government to achieve its macroeconomic objectives. **[12]**
▸ Show mark guidance

(a) Market-based: tax cuts, deregulation, privatisation, labour reform — sharpen incentives. Interventionist: education, infrastructure, R&D — state builds capacity. Two examples each.

(b) For: only LRAS growth improves all four objectives simultaneously and without inflation. Against: decade-long lags (useless in a recession), cost, uncertain success, inequality from market-based variants. Judgement: essential for the long run, but demand-side tools remain necessary for stabilisation — a mix, sequenced by the problem at hand.
